item.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, Veeam CVE-2026-44963, and Miasma Supply-Chain Fallout
item.description: During this coverage window, reporting was dominated by large-scale vulnerability remediation, software supply-chain compromise, and the accelerating role of AI in vulnerability discovery. Microsoft addressed 200 flaws including three publicly disclosed zero-days, while Veeam patched a critical...
item.content:encoded: During this coverage window, reporting was dominated by large-scale vulnerability remediation, software supply-chain compromise, and the accelerating role of AI in vulnerability discovery. Microsoft addressed 200 flaws including three publicly disclosed zero-days, while Veeam patched a critical Backup & Replication RCE issue affecting domain-joined servers. Separate reporting detailed fallout from the Miasma/Shai-Hulud ecosystem and new malware and authentication research relevant to defenders. Microsoft's June 2026 Patch Tuesday fixed 200 vulnerabilities, including 33 critical issues and three publicly disclosed zero-days: CVE-2026-45586 in Windows Collaborative Translation Framework, CVE-2026-49160 in HTTP.sys, and CVE-2026-50507 in Windows BitLocker. Microsoft said none of the three zero-days were known to have been exploited. The release also introduced a MaxHeadersCount registry setting tied to mitigation of the HTTP/2 Bomb denial-of-service issue affecting HTTP.sys. Microsoft also released mandatory Windows 11 cumulative updates KB5094126 and KB5093998 for supported 25H2/24H2 and 23H2 systems. The updates include security fixes, reliability improvements, and feature changes such as Shared Audio enhancements, Task Manager NPU visibility, Multi-App Camera policy controls, and setup-time custom user folder naming. Microsoft reported no new known issues for this Patch Tuesday release. Veeam released fixes for CVE-2026-44963, a critical Backup & Replication vulnerability that can allow remote code execution on domain-joined backup servers. The issue affects version 12.3.2.4465 and earlier version 12 builds and is fixed in 12.3.2.4854, while version 13.x is not affected due to architectural changes. Veeam said exploitation requires any low-privileged authenticated domain user and noted there were no reports of active exploitation. Microsoft confirmed that some GitHub repositories were temporarily removed after a security incident involving 73 open-source projects compromised to inject an information stealer. Some repositories were restored after review, while others remained offline as the investigation continued. Microsoft said a small number of customers who may have pulled affected content were notified through support channels. Earlier reporting said the removed repositories spanned Azure, microsoft, Azure-Samples, and MicrosoftDocs organizations and briefly disrupted CI workflows, including Azure/functions-action usage. Researchers linked the incident to broader Miasma/Shai-Hulud supply-chain activity involving Microsoft GitHub resources, Red Hat npm packages, PyPI packages, and durabletask. Analysis of the Miasma payload found it could trigger automatic code execution when an affected repository was opened in an AI-powered coding tool or IDE. StepSecurity introduced a Threat Center API endpoint that returns compromised open source components associated with a specific incident. The endpoint provides ecosystem, component, affected version, severity, verification status, description, and threat intelligence identifiers. The company described use cases including SIEM enrichment, SOC workflow automation, and correlation against SBOMs or lockfiles. XBOW reported testing Anthropic's Mythos Preview across offensive security benchmarks, workflows, integrations, and API-based agents. The testing found the model stronger than prior systems at identifying vulnerability candidates, particularly with source code access, while live-site interaction remained important for exploitability validation. XBOW also reported strong results in native-code vulnerability discovery, reverse engineering, and browser-based visual workflows, with mixed judgment and high estimated cost. Mitiga highlighted Anthropic reporting that Project Glasswing partners used Mythos Preview to find more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities in systemically important software. The article argued that the bottleneck has shifted from discovery to verification, disclosure, and patching, citing 75 patched issues out of 530 disclosed high- or critical-severity bugs at the time of writing. It also framed modern exposure around pre-patch exploitation timelines and very short eCrime breakout times. Health-ISAC discussed President Trump's AI executive order through a healthcare cybersecurity lens. Errol Weiss said the sector's opportunity lies in faster coordination, especially as attackers use AI to compress the time from vulnerability discovery to exploitation. The article emphasized timely, actionable information sharing through ISAC channels and measurable resilience improvements for critical infrastructure. Zscaler ThreatLabz analyzed MLTBackdoor, a new malware family identified in May 2026 and assessed as likely connected to a ransomware-related actor. The malware was delivered through a multi-stage ClickFix chain using endpointdlp.dll and an RC4-encrypted data.bin payload. It uses heavy obfuscation, API hashing, indirect system calls, anti-analysis checks, filesystem commands, a Beacon Object File loader, and encrypted TLS-based command-and-control over port 443. SpecterOps published research on Kerberos user-to-user authentication in Windows environments. The article explains how U2U encrypts a service ticket with a target user's TGT session key, including the KERB-TGT-REQUEST and ENC-TKT-IN-SKEY flow. It also details RDP Network Level Authentication usage during CredSSP negotiation and how UnPAC-the-Hash combines PKINIT and U2U to recover a user's NT hash from PACCREDENTIALINFO.

item.title: CVE-2026-11645, CVE-2025-8088, SiribClone, Tchap Breach Drive Threat Digest
item.description: This digest highlights active exploitation, espionage targeting military personnel, account hijacking, and abuse of trusted identity and collaboration workflows. Several reports show attackers relying less on novel malware alone and more on legitimate platforms, OAuth flows, cloud services,...
item.content:encoded: This digest highlights active exploitation, espionage targeting military personnel, account hijacking, and abuse of trusted identity and collaboration workflows. Several reports show attackers relying less on novel malware alone and more on legitimate platforms, OAuth flows, cloud services, residential proxy infrastructure, and social engineering to gain access or evade detection. A previously undocumented espionage group tracked as SiribClone has targeted Russian military personnel since at least summer 2025, particularly in border regions and combat zones. The group posed as women seeking romantic relationships or as humanitarian volunteers to persuade servicemen to install malicious Android applications or enter Telegram credentials into spoofed sites. SiribClone’s Android spyware, SafeLoveStealer, collected files, location data, and other information from infected devices, and could remotely activate microphones to record conversations. F6 researchers also identified an internal platform called Kontur that stored stolen Telegram sessions and included notes referencing ranks, unit designations, locations, and operational status. Separately, Trend Micro attributed exploitation of the WinRAR path traversal flaw CVE-2025-8088 against Ukrainian organizations to two Russia-aligned clusters: Earth Dahu, also known as Gamaredon, and SHADOW-EARTH-066, also known as UAC-0226. SHADOW-EARTH-066 used crafted RAR files with hidden NTFS Alternate Data Streams to deploy the GIFTEDCROOK stealer, while Earth Dahu used an HTA-to-VBScript chain delivering GammaPhish, GammaLoad, and GammaSteel components. AppOmni detailed how device code phishing abuses the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant to obtain valid access and refresh tokens without stealing passwords. The technique has expanded beyond Microsoft 365 to SaaS platforms including Salesforce and GitHub, and can bypass expectations around MFA, FIDO2, and passkeys because victims authenticate through legitimate provider workflows. Varonis Threat Labs tested an OpenClaw AI inbox agent named Pinchy and found that, in two simulated scenarios, the agent forwarded sensitive material such as AWS IAM keys, database passwords, SSH credentials, and a CRM export to external requesters. The research framed this as agent phishing, where plausible requests exploit an agent’s delegated trust, rather than indirect prompt injection through malicious embedded instructions. IRONSCALES reported a Google Forms editor invite sent to a multinational law firm through Google infrastructure that passed SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and composite authentication checks. The sender display name matched the recipient’s name, while the Hebrew-language form referenced a property tax rights survey and used editor invite links that did not require Google sign-in. DINUM disclosed that France’s government encrypted messaging platform Tchap was breached through a compromised user account. ANSSI detected the incident, DINUM blocked the malicious account, and the French data protection authority CNIL was notified because personal data in accessible conversations may have been exposed. A threat actor claimed responsibility for the Tchap incident and alleged social engineering was used to access an education shard and scrape messages, files, and account information. DINUM also noted that Tchap public rooms can be joined by any user and are not encrypted, distinguishing them from protected private communications. Infoblox reported broad enterprise exposure to residential proxy infrastructure, finding that more than 65% of its Threat Defense Cloud customers made DNS queries in 2026 to domains used to access or orchestrate such networks. Monthly DNS queries to residential proxy domains grew from nearly 400 billion in January 2025 to more than 500 billion by April 2026, with Brightdata the most frequently observed service across customer environments. Google released Chrome updates addressing 74 vulnerabilities, including the actively exploited V8 zero-day CVE-2026-11645. The high-severity out-of-bounds memory access flaw could allow remote code execution inside the Chrome sandbox via a crafted HTML page in versions prior to 149.0.7827.103. University of Toronto researchers demonstrated a proof-of-concept self-replicating AI worm using a locally hosted open-weight large language model. In isolated tests on a deliberately vulnerable 33-host network, the worm autonomously identified vulnerabilities, gained elevated access, replicated across hosts, and in some cases rewrote its own code to bypass local security controls. A separate analysis of modern network security operations described risk arising from fragmented work between tools rather than from a simple lack of detections. The article characterized manual coordination across SIEM, firewalls, IAM, ITSM, cloud, monitoring, and messaging systems as a source of delays, drift, audit gaps, overprivileged access, and inconsistent enforcement.

item.title: CVE-2026-50751, CVE-2026-11645, and CVE-2026-42271 Exploited in the Wild
item.description: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of newly disclosed or recently patched vulnerabilities across remote access infrastructure, browser software, and AI application tooling. The highest-impact reporting centers on bugs that can enable unauthorized access, code execution, or...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of newly disclosed or recently patched vulnerabilities across remote access infrastructure, browser software, and AI application tooling. The highest-impact reporting centers on bugs that can enable unauthorized access, code execution, or post-compromise activity, with CISA adding urgency through federal remediation deadlines and Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog entries. A common theme is adversary focus on exposed access paths: VPN gateways, browser attack surface, and developer-facing AI proxy services. The reported exploitation includes ransomware-linked post-compromise activity, Chrome zero-day use in the wild, and a LiteLLM flaw that can be chained into unauthenticated remote code execution under certain conditions. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to secure affected Check Point devices against CVE-2026-50751 by June 11 after the flaw was exploited as a zero-day. The vulnerability allows unauthenticated remote attackers to bypass authentication and establish a remote access VPN connection on targeted Check Point Mobile Access/SSL VPNs, Remote Access VPNs, or Spark firewalls. The exposure is limited to specific legacy or weakened configurations, including deployments using the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol, gateways that do not require a machine certificate, and legacy Remote Access clients. Check Point reported exploitation beginning on May 7, with activity surging over the weekend and affecting a few dozen targeted organizations globally. The company also linked at least one post-compromise incident involving CVE-2026-50751 to a Qilin ransomware affiliate, elevating the issue from access abuse to ransomware-enabled intrusion risk. CISA also added CVE-2026-42271 in BerriAI LiteLLM to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog based on evidence of active exploitation. The flaw is a high-severity command injection issue affecting LiteLLM Python package versions 1.74.2 through versions earlier than 1.83.7, allowing any authenticated user to execute arbitrary commands on the host. Horizon3.ai reported that CVE-2026-42271 can be chained with CVE-2026-48710, a Starlette host header validation bypass, to achieve unauthenticated remote code execution against vulnerable LiteLLM deployments. BerriAI addressed the issue in LiteLLM 1.83.7 by requiring the PROXYADMIN role for two MCP server preview test endpoints, while the reported chain highlights how AI infrastructure components can become externally exploitable when combined with framework-level weaknesses. Google released emergency Chrome updates for CVE-2026-11645, a high-severity zero-day vulnerability in the V8 JavaScript engine that the company said has been exploited in the wild. The patched Stable Desktop versions are 149.0.7827.102 for Windows and Linux and 149.0.7827.103 for Mac. CVE-2026-11645 is described as an out-of-bounds read and write weakness exploitable through crafted HTML pages. Successful exploitation can allow remote attackers to execute arbitrary code inside Chrome’s sandbox, access data beyond the intended memory buffer through heap corruption, expose sensitive information, or trigger browser crashes. Google said access to bug details and related links may remain restricted until a majority of users have received the fix, consistent with its handling of actively exploited browser vulnerabilities.

item.title: Digest 2026-06-09 00:00 UTC
item.content:encoded:
Microsoft Patch Tuesday for June 2026 — Snort rules and prominent vulnerabilities — Must Read

Microsoft's June 2026 Patch Tuesday addresses 206 vulnerabilities, including 32 rated critical, with notable flaws in Remote Desktop Client, Windows HTTP Protocol Stack (http.sys), and the Windows Graphics component flagged by Microsoft as more likely to be exploited. Cisco Talos has released Snort 2 and Snort 3 rulesets to detect attempts to exploit several of these vulnerabilities.

  • Microsoft's June 2026 security update addresses 206 vulnerabilities, 32 of which are rated critical, including 28 critical remote code execution vulnerabilities across Windows services and applications.
  • Talos highlighted four critical vulnerabilities Microsoft deemed 'more likely' to be exploited: CVE-2026-42985 (Remote Desktop Client heap-based buffer overflow), CVE-2026-47291 (Windows HTTP Protocol Stack integer overflow), and CVE-2026-44803 and CVE-2026-44812 (Windows Graphics component integer overflow).
  • Several critical Outlook and Word vulnerabilities (CVE-2026-45456, CVE-2026-45458, and CVE-2026-47635) can be exploited via the preview pane of Outlook (classic), which renders emails using Microsoft Word functionality where a type confusion flaw exists.
  • Microsoft also flagged 'important' vulnerabilities considered 'more likely' to be exploited, including elevation of privilege flaws in the Windows DWM Core Library, NT OS Kernel, Winlogon, and SharePoint Server spoofing vulnerabilities.
  • Talos released new Snort rulesets to detect exploitation attempts, including Snort 2 rules 66572-66577, 66581, 66589, 66590, 66594, 66595, 66601-66604 and Snort 3 rules 301523-301525, 301527-301529, 301531, and 301532.

SAP fixes critical flaws in NetWeaver and Commerce Cloud — Must Read

SAP released its June 2026 Security Patch package fixing 15 vulnerabilities, including four critical flaws in SAP NetWeaver and Commerce Cloud. The most severe are an XML Signature Wrapping authentication bypass (CVE-2026-44748, CVSS 9.9) and an unauthenticated memory corruption flaw (CVE-2026-27671, CVSS 9.8), which organizations should prioritize patching.

  • SAP released fixes for 15 vulnerabilities as part of its June 2026 Security Patch package, including four critical-severity flaws affecting SAP NetWeaver and SAP Commerce Cloud.
  • CVE-2026-44748 (CVSS 9.9) is an XML Signature Wrapping flaw in SAP NetWeaver AS ABAP and ABAP Platform that could allow authentication bypass in SAML-based environments by enabling an authenticated attacker to send modified signed XML documents.
  • CVE-2026-27671 (CVSS 9.8) is a memory corruption flaw in SAP NetWeaver/ABAP Platform Application Server ABAP that can be exploited without authentication via crafted RFC requests leveraging improper kernel validation.
  • Additional critical flaws include CVE-2026-22732 (CVSS 9.1), a Spring Security-related vulnerability in SAP Commerce Cloud and SAP Data Hub, and CVE-2026-40128 (CVSS 9.0), a directory traversal vulnerability in SAP NetWeaver Application Server Java's Web Container.
  • SAP also addressed two high-severity vulnerabilities and various SQL injection, path traversal, cross-site scripting, email spoofing, and authorization bypass issues, with full details available only to SAP customers with a security portal account.

Microsoft’s June 2026 Patch Tuesday Addresses 198 CVEs ( CVE-2026-49160, CVE-2026-50507) — Must Read

Microsoft's June 2026 Patch Tuesday is its largest ever, fixing 198 CVEs (32 critical) including three publicly disclosed zero-days: a BitLocker bypass (CVE-2026-50507 'Bitskrieg'), an HTTP.sys DoS (CVE-2026-49160 'HTTP/2 Bomb'), and a CTFMON privilege escalation (CVE-2026-45586). It also references out-of-band fixes, notably the actively exploited, CISA KEV-listed Defender EoP flaw CVE-2026-41091 ('RedSun').

  • Microsoft's June 2026 Patch Tuesday addressed 198 CVEs, with 32 rated critical and 166 rated important, making it the largest Patch Tuesday release ever and surpassing the previous record of 167 CVEs from October 2025.
  • Three zero-day vulnerabilities were publicly disclosed prior to patches being available: CVE-2026-50507 (a BitLocker security feature bypass requiring physical access, known as Bitskrieg), CVE-2026-49160 (an HTTP.sys denial of service affecting HTTP/2 dubbed HTTP/2 Bomb), and CVE-2026-45586 (a Windows Collaborative Translation Framework elevation of privilege granting SYSTEM privileges).
  • Eleven Remote Desktop Client RCE vulnerabilities were patched that could be triggered by a victim connecting to an attacker-controlled server, causing a heap-based buffer overflow, with CVE-2026-42985 assessed as Exploitation More Likely.
  • An out-of-band Microsoft Defender elevation of privilege vulnerability (CVE-2026-41091, known as RedSun) was disclosed by a researcher called Chaotic Eclipse, has been exploited in the wild, and was added to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 20, 2026.
  • Elevation of Privilege vulnerabilities accounted for 31.8% of the patched flaws while remote code execution vulnerabilities accounted for 27.3%.

Microsoft June 2026 Patch Tuesday fixes 3 zero-day, 200 flaws — Must Read

Microsoft's June 2026 Patch Tuesday fixes 200 vulnerabilities including 33 critical flaws and three publicly disclosed zero-days: a Windows CTFMON privilege escalation bug, the HTTP/2 Bomb denial-of-service flaw (CVE-2026-49160), and the YellowKey BitLocker bypass (CVE-2026-50507). None of the zero-days are known to have been exploited in attacks.

  • Microsoft's June 2026 Patch Tuesday addresses 200 flaws, including 33 critical vulnerabilities (28 remote code execution, 4 elevation of privilege, and 1 information disclosure) and three publicly disclosed zero-day vulnerabilities, none of which are known to have been exploited in attacks.
  • CVE-2026-45586 is a publicly disclosed Windows Collaborative Translation Framework (CTFMON) elevation of privilege flaw that grants SYSTEM privileges through improper link resolution, credited to an anonymous researcher.
  • CVE-2026-49160, called the 'HTTP/2 Bomb,' is a denial-of-service flaw disclosed by researchers at offensive security firm Calif that abuses HTTP/2 header compression to force servers to allocate disproportionately large amounts of memory, prompting Microsoft to introduce a new 'MaxHeadersCount' registry setting as mitigation.
  • CVE-2026-50507 is a Windows BitLocker security feature bypass that BleepingComputer identified as the 'YellowKey' vulnerability disclosed by researcher Nightmare Eclipse, exploitable via crafted files on a USB drive or EFI partition and primarily affecting TPM-only BitLocker systems on Windows 11 and Windows Server 2022/2025.
  • The roundup excludes flaws fixed earlier in the month for products like Mariner, Azure HorizonDB, and Microsoft Copilot, as well as 360 Microsoft Edge/Chromium flaws fixed by Google, and notes that other vendors including Acer, Check Point, Cisco, Fortinet, Google, Ivanti, Ubiquiti, SAP, and Veeam also released security updates.

Wait, binding.gyp Can Do What? Exploring npm's Weirdest Build System — Must Read

The Miasma npm worm has evolved to hide its install-time code execution inside binding.gyp files rather than package.json hooks, exploiting node-gyp's command expansions, Python eval sandbox escapes, auto-included files, compiler hijacking, and build actions. This means a package with a completely clean package.json can still run malicious code during npm install, making such files require the same scrutiny as preinstall/postinstall hooks.

  • The latest variant of the Miasma worm moved its install-time trigger out of package.json hooks and into binding.gyp, which npm automatically executes via node-gyp rebuild whenever the file is present in a package's root, even with a completely clean package.json.
  • GYP supports command expansion syntax such as <!(command) that runs shell commands at parse time, and because GYP walks the entire parsed structure recursively, these commands execute under any key name at any depth, not just documented fields like sources or include_dirs.
  • Because binding.gyp is parsed with Python's eval using stripped builtins, attackers can use a sandbox escape (starting from an empty tuple and traversing object subclasses to catch_warnings) to import the os module and run arbitrary shell commands, hiding pure Python payloads in normal-looking build files or condition strings.
  • Payloads can be hidden in files pulled in through the includes key, through transitively auto-included files like config.gypi, common.gypi, and the persistent ~/.gyp/include.gypi in the user's home directory, or through target dependencies pointing at other gyp files.
  • Attackers can hijack the build by replacing the compiler through make_global_settings (CC, CXX, *_wrapper keys) or injecting malicious $(shell ...) commands into compiler flags, and can also execute code directly through GYP's documented actions, rules, and postbuilds features without needing any command expansion syntax.

New Veeam vulnerability exposes backup servers to RCE attacks — Must Read

Veeam has patched a critical vulnerability (CVE-2026-44963) in Backup & Replication that lets any low-privileged domain user achieve remote code execution on domain-joined backup servers. The flaw affects VBR 12.3.2.4465 and earlier version 12 builds (fixed in 12.3.2.4854), only impacts domain-joined installations, and users should patch promptly as Veeam backup servers are frequently targeted by ransomware gangs.

  • Veeam released security updates to patch a critical vulnerability (CVE-2026-44963) in Backup & Replication that allows an authenticated domain user to achieve remote code execution on domain-joined backup servers.
  • The flaw affects Veeam Backup & Replication 12.3.2.4465 and all earlier version 12 builds, was fixed in version 12.3.2.4854, and does not affect any version 13.x build due to architectural changes.
  • The vulnerability only impacts Veeam installations joined to a domain, a configuration that contradicts Veeam's long-standing hardening best practices, yet many companies have joined their servers to a Windows domain.
  • There are no reports of active exploitation, but Veeam warned that attackers will likely attempt to reverse-engineer the patch to exploit unpatched deployments, and the vulnerability was reported by WatchTowr researcher Sina Kheirkhah.
  • Veeam backup servers are frequently targeted by ransomware gangs such as Akira, Fog, Frag, and Cuba, as well as the FIN7 group, and CISA has flagged four previously exploited Veeam Backup & Replication flaws.

Veeam Backup & Replication RCE Flaw Lets Domain Users Run Remote Code — Must Read

Veeam has patched a critical RCE vulnerability (CVE-2026-44963, CVSS 9.4) in Backup & Replication that allows authenticated domain users to execute remote code on the Backup Server, affecting version 12.3.2.4465 and earlier 12.x builds but not 13.x. Users should update to version 12.3.2.4854, especially since prior Veeam flaws have been exploited by ransomware groups.

  • Veeam has released security patches for a critical vulnerability tracked as CVE-2026-44963, which carries a CVSS score of 9.4 and allows remote code execution on the Backup Server by an authenticated domain user.
  • The flaw impacts Veeam Backup & Replication version 12.3.2.4465 and all earlier 12.x builds, but does not affect any 13.x build due to architectural changes introduced in version 13.
  • The vulnerability has been addressed in Veeam Backup & Replication version 12.3.2.4854.
  • watchTowr researcher Sina Kheirkhah was credited for responsibly discovering and reporting the issue.
  • In March 2026, Veeam resolved multiple critical vulnerabilities in Backup & Replication, and prior vulnerabilities in the software have been exploited by bad actors, including ransomware groups.

Windows 11 KB5094126 & KB5093998 cumulative updates released — Must Read

Microsoft released mandatory Windows 11 cumulative updates KB5094126 (for 25H2/24H2) and KB5093998 (for 23H2) delivering the June 2026 Patch Tuesday security fixes along with new features like Shared Audio, Xbox mode, and Task Manager NPU monitoring. Microsoft reports no known issues with this relatively small release.

  • Microsoft released the mandatory Windows 11 KB5094126 (for versions 25H2/24H2) and KB5093998 (for 23H2) cumulative updates containing the June 2026 Patch Tuesday security patches, with build numbers changing to 26200.8457 (25H2), 26100.8457 (24H2), and 22631.7079 (23H2).
  • The updates introduce new features including Shared Audio, which lets two people listen to the same audio from one PC via Bluetooth LE Audio broadcast, and expanded Xbox mode availability on more PCs.
  • Task Manager now offers improved NPU usage visibility through new optional NPU columns and an Isolation column showing which apps run in an AppContainer.
  • The updates improve Windows Hello reliability, including optimizing the Windows Biometric service performance and reducing unexpected authentication blocks in Enhanced Sign-in Security.
  • Microsoft is not aware of any new issues with this month's Patch Tuesday, attributing this to it being a smaller release and its commitment to a stable and reliable Windows experience.

GitHub disables Microsoft repos pushing password-stealing malware — Must Read

Microsoft temporarily removed 73 GitHub repositories across its Azure, Microsoft, Azure-Samples, and MicrosoftDocs organizations on June 5 after they were compromised in the Miasma/Shai-Hulud supply-chain campaign, briefly disrupting CI pipelines and the widely-used Azure/functions-action. All repositories have been restored and deemed clean, but Microsoft notified a small number of customers who may have pulled affected content.

  • On June 5, Microsoft removed 73 repositories across its Azure, microsoft, Azure-Samples, and MicrosoftDocs GitHub organizations after concerns they distributed potentially malicious content, with the action contained within 105 seconds.
  • Researchers confirmed the repositories were pulled following a compromise during the Miasma/Shai-Hulud supply-chain campaign, which targeted AI coding tools like Claude Code, Gemini CLI, VS Code, and Cursor.
  • The most significant immediate effect was disabling access to the 'Azure/functions-action' GitHub Action used by many developers to deploy Azure Functions, causing an outage and confusion for workflows referencing it.
  • According to Cloudsmith, the attacker pivoted from Red Hat's npm packages to Microsoft's GitHub resources by compromising a Red Hat employee's GitHub account and injecting a workflow that requested GitHub's OIDC tokens.
  • All affected repositories have been restored and are considered clean and safe, and Microsoft notified a small number of customers who may have pulled down content from the compromised repositories.

WinRAR Flaw Exploited by Russia-Aligned Groups to Deploy Stealers in Ukraine — Should Read

Two Russia-aligned groups, Gamaredon (Earth Dahu) and UAC-0226 (SHADOW-EARTH-066), are still exploiting the patched WinRAR flaw CVE-2025-8088 to deploy information stealers like GIFTEDCROOK and GammaSteel against Ukrainian organizations nearly a year after a fix was released. The campaigns abuse NTFS Alternate Data Streams to plant hidden payloads, highlighting how unpatched software keeps an exploited entry point open long after the patch ships.

  • Two Russia-aligned groups, Earth Dahu (Gamaredon) and SHADOW-EARTH-066 (UAC-0226), continue to exploit CVE-2025-8088, a WinRAR path traversal flaw using NTFS Alternate Data Streams, nearly a year after it was patched in July 2025.
  • SHADOW-EARTH-066 uses crafted RAR archives with a decoy PDF and three hidden ADS payloads, including an LNK file placed in the Startup folder that spawns a PowerShell loader to ultimately launch an updated version of the GIFTEDCROOK information stealer.
  • GIFTEDCROOK targets passwords and cookies from Chromium-based browsers and Firefox, harvests documents matching certain extensions, and deletes all malicious artifacts after exfiltration; it has shifted from Telegram to dedicated C2 servers following Russia's blocking of Telegram in February.
  • Earth Dahu has used the vulnerability since at least September 2025 via an HTA-to-VBScript infection chain that delivers GammaPhish, GammaLoad, and the GammaSteel information stealer, with activity remaining active through at least April 10, 2026.
  • Trend Micro notes that WinRAR is deeply embedded in Ukrainian organizations' daily operations, making it an attractive target, and the convergence of multiple state-backed and independent groups on a single vulnerability reflects the scale of cyber threats facing Ukraine.

Microsoft Restores Some GitHub Repos, Keeps Others Offline as Miasma Probe Continues — Should Read

Microsoft temporarily removed some of its GitHub repositories after 73 of its open-source projects were compromised in the ongoing Miasma software supply chain campaign to inject information stealers, restoring some repos after review while others remain offline. The broader campaign, linked to the Shai-Hulud and Hades waves, continues to evolve with new payload delivery methods targeting PyPI bioinformatics and MCP/AI-themed packages, including malware capable of triggering automatic code execution in AI-powered IDEs and bypassing AI scanners via prompt injection.

  • Microsoft confirmed it temporarily removed some GitHub repositories in response to a security incident that led to 73 of its open-source projects being compromised to inject an information stealer, restoring some repos after review while others remain offline.
  • Microsoft notified a small number of customers who may have pulled down content from the affected repositories and said it will reach out directly if further customer action is required.
  • Among the infected projects was 'durabletask,' a Python package first compromised by the cybercrime group TeamPCP to deliver an information stealer targeting Linux systems.
  • The Miasma payload can trigger automatic code execution when a developer opens the repository in an AI-powered coding tool or IDE, and a newer PyPI wave tied to Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades infected 23 additional packages, including bioinformatics and MCP/AI-themed and typosquatted packages.
  • The new package cluster uses evolving payload delivery mechanisms such as trojanized native .abi3.so extensions and a .pth loader that searches sys.path for a separate _index.js payload, while the malware harvests secrets from developer workstations and CI/CD environments and exfiltrates them to a public GitHub repository.

ServiceNow discloses security incident exposing customer data — Should Read

ServiceNow disclosed a security incident in which attackers exploited an unauthenticated API endpoint (reportedly /api/now/related_list_edit/create) to query data from customer instances, prompting a June 5, 2026 security update that restricted the endpoint to authenticated users. The flaw mainly affected customers on the Australia platform release or those with certain configuration changes on older releases, and ServiceNow has notified impacted customers directly.

  • ServiceNow disclosed a security incident in which attackers exploited an unauthenticated access flaw through a vulnerable API endpoint to query data from customer instances, and the company applied a security update to hosted customer instances on June 5, 2026.
  • The security update changed the API endpoint configuration to limit access to authenticated users only, and ServiceNow confirmed that attackers successfully queried customer instance tables.
  • Administrators discussing the incident on Reddit indicated the issue appears tied to a REST endpoint at /api/now/related_list_edit/create that was reportedly configured with requires_authentication=false, with the update setting it to true, and shared indicators of compromise including the IP address 51.159.98.241.
  • The bulletin states the issue primarily impacts customers running the Australia platform release or those on older releases who made certain configuration changes, and ServiceNow has opened support cases with affected customers while those not contacted are believed to be unaffected.
  • ServiceNow has not disclosed which data was accessed and is still evaluating whether to publish a CVE, and impacted organizations are advised to review exposed records, rotate credentials shared through support workflows, and ensure API logging is enabled.

Microsoft releases Windows 10 KB5094127 extended security update — Should Read

Microsoft released the Windows 10 KB5094127 extended security update, which patches the June 2026 Patch Tuesday vulnerabilities (200 flaws including three zero-days) and adds Secure Boot certificate rollout monitoring, bringing systems to build 19045.7417. Microsoft also warns of a known issue where recent updates can trigger BitLocker recovery prompts on devices using a PCR7 TPM validation Group Policy, with a temporary workaround available while a permanent fix is developed.

  • Microsoft released the Windows 10 KB5094127 extended security update, which is available to users running Windows 10 Enterprise LTSC or those enrolled in the ESU program, and updates systems to build 19045.7417 (or 19044.7417 for Enterprise LTSC 2021).
  • The update includes fixes from the June 2026 Patch Tuesday, which addressed 200 vulnerabilities including three publicly disclosed zero-day flaws.
  • KB5094127 improves File Explorer search and adds Secure Boot enhancements, including dynamic status reporting, a new LimitSecureBootRequiredServiceData policy setting, and additional device targeting data for the phased rollout of new Secure Boot certificates.
  • Microsoft warns of a known issue that can trigger BitLocker recovery prompts on devices configured with a BitLocker Group Policy that explicitly includes PCR7 in the TPM validation profile along with certain Secure Boot and Windows Boot Manager configurations tied to the Windows UEFI CA 2023 certificate.
  • As a temporary workaround for the BitLocker issue, Microsoft advises removing the Group Policy setting and then suspending and resuming BitLocker to regenerate the default PCR bindings while it works on a permanent fix.

Technical Analysis of MLTBackdoor — Should Read

Zscaler ThreatLabz analyzed MLTBackdoor, a newly identified and heavily obfuscated backdoor likely tied to ransomware activity that is delivered through a ClickFix infection chain. Its key risk is that it maintains resilient C2 access via DGA and encrypted communications while using a BOF loader to dynamically expand post-exploitation capabilities.

  • Zscaler ThreatLabz identified a new malware family called MLTBackdoor in May 2026 that is likely leveraged by a ransomware-related threat actor.
  • MLTBackdoor was observed being delivered through a multi-stage ClickFix infection chain that downloads an archive containing endpointdlp.dll and an RC4-encrypted data.bin payload.
  • MLTBackdoor is heavily obfuscated with Mixed Boolean-Arithmetic and Control Flow Flattening techniques and also uses stack-built strings, API hashing, indirect system calls, and multiple anti-analysis checks.
  • MLTBackdoor supports filesystem commands such as download, upload, ls, delete, rename, and mkdir, and includes a Beacon Object File loader to dynamically add capabilities.
  • MLTBackdoor uses a custom encrypted binary protocol over TLS on port 443 and can rely on hardcoded C2 domains, a domain generation algorithm, or both for command-and-control communication.

Researchers Build Self-Replicating AI Worm That Operates Entirely on Local, Open-Weight Models — Should Read

University of Toronto researchers demonstrated a self-replicating AI worm that uses local open-weight LLMs to autonomously find, exploit, and spread across vulnerable hosts without relying on commercial AI APIs. The key warning is that adaptive malware can turn new public advisories into attack paths during the patching window, making segmentation, rapid patching, and host-level containment critical.

  • University of Toronto researchers built and tested a proof-of-concept AI-driven computer worm that uses a locally hosted open-weight large language model to generate target-specific attack strategies and replicate without human intervention or commercial AI services.
  • In 15 isolated runs on a deliberately vulnerable 33-host network, the worm identified an average of 31.3 vulnerabilities, gained elevated access on 23.1 hosts, and replicated autonomously to 20.4 hosts over seven days.
  • The worm ingested public advisory text at runtime and successfully exploited test hosts configured with vulnerabilities disclosed after the model was trained, including CVE-2026-39987, CVE-2026-31431, and DirtyFrag-related Linux kernel issues.
  • The researchers observed the worm rewrite its own code on several occasions to bypass local security controls in the test environment, although the prototype was deliberately built without stealth, persistence, encryption, or log-cleanup features.
  • The article recommends defenders segment GPU-capable machines, prioritize rapid handling of internet-facing CVEs, rotate credentials exposed on compromised hosts, and monitor for agent-specific signals such as unexpected LLM inference and automated SSH public key injection.

Device Code Phishing Explained: How Attackers Abuse OAuth Across SaaS — Should Read

Device code phishing abuses the legitimate OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant to steal access and refresh tokens, bypassing MFA and passkeys because victims complete authentication on real identity provider infrastructure. While first seen against Microsoft 365, attackers like Storm-2372 and Scattered Lapsus$ Hunters have expanded the technique to Salesforce, GitHub, and other SaaS platforms, making consent-layer visibility and OAuth monitoring essential since the attack survives even when individual device flows are removed.

  • Device code phishing abuses the legitimate OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628) to obtain valid access and refresh tokens without stealing a victim's password, because the victim completes authentication and MFA on the real identity provider while unknowingly authorizing an attacker-controlled device.
  • MFA, FIDO2, and passkeys do not fully stop the attack since the authentication flow is legitimate end-to-end, and password resets do not revoke the issued refresh tokens, which can remain valid for weeks or months and grant persistent access.
  • The technique is platform-agnostic and has been used beyond Microsoft 365, including the Russia-aligned Storm-2372 actor against Microsoft 365 tenants and the Scattered Lapsus$ Hunters alliance combining vishing with device code phishing against Salesforce, which claimed over 1,000 organizations compromised and roughly 1.5 billion records stolen.
  • Salesforce responded by removing OAuth 2.0 device flow entirely in September 2025, but the same threat actors shifted to standard OAuth authorization code grants, demonstrating that the attack vector survives specific countermeasures and defenses must focus on the consent layer.
  • Because the attack is invisible to email, URL reputation, and adversary-in-the-middle defenses, security teams should focus on identity-layer telemetry, OAuth grant and connected application inventory, consent governance, and restricting unnecessary OAuth authorization paths.

Residential Proxies in the Wild — Should Read

Infoblox found that over 65% of its Threat Defense Cloud customers queried domains tied to residential proxy networks in 2026, with such traffic growing ~25% to over 500 billion queries monthly and appearing across high-risk verticals like government, banking, pharmaceutical, and healthcare. These proxies—often installed non-consensually via proxyware in VPNs, free apps, and IoT devices—expose corporate IP space to abuse, legal exposure, and internal network probing, prompting recommendations to use Protective DNS and audit installed apps and DNS logs.

  • Infoblox found that over 65% of its Threat Defense Cloud customers made DNS queries to domains used to access or orchestrate residential proxy networks in 2026, with monthly queries growing about 25% over the year to more than 500 billion.
  • Residential proxies route traffic through everyday consumer devices and produce laundered traffic that appears to come from real residential users, making them valuable to attackers for evading IP reputation systems, bypassing fraud detection, and blending abuse into normal consumer noise.
  • Proxyware is frequently embedded via SDKs into free applications such as VPNs, streaming apps, screensavers, and productivity tools, as well as low-cost IoT devices, often enrolling device owners into proxy networks without their knowledge.
  • Brightdata was the most frequently observed proxy service, appearing in over 50% of cloud customers, while other notable services included Oxylabs, Hola VPN, Honeygain, and Grass, the latter reportedly pre-installed on Superbox Android TV streaming devices.
  • Over 90% of Infoblox's pharmaceutical and food and beverage customers and over 60% of government and banking customers queried residential proxy indicators, prompting recommendations to use Protective DNS, review DNS query logs, and audit installed applications and IoT devices.

Phishing for Lobsters: How We Tricked OpenClaw into Spilling Secrets — Should Read

Varonis Threat Labs tested an OpenClaw AI email agent against classic phishing simulations and found that while it reliably caught technical attacks like malicious OAuth flows and fake login portals, it failed at social-context attacks, readily forwarding AWS keys, database passwords, and a CRM export to external attackers who simply posed as a trusted colleague. The takeaway is that AI agents are stronger than humans at spotting technical phishing but lack the instinctive identity-verification and social suspicion needed to resist plausible business-pretext requests, requiring architectural defenses like human-in-the-loop approvals and connector segmentation.

  • Varonis Threat Labs built an OpenClaw AI email agent named Pinchy and tested it against four phishing simulations using Google Gemini 3.1 Pro and OpenAI Codex GPT-5.4 models under both Generic and Strict configuration profiles.
  • In Case Study 1, a casual email from an external Gmail account impersonating a team lead named 'Dan' tricked the agent into forwarding AWS IAM access keys, database connection strings, and SSH credentials in plaintext, and in Case Study 2 it exfiltrated a CRM export containing 247 enterprise customers and roughly $1.28M in monthly recurring revenue data.
  • The agent reliably handled technical phishing such as malicious OAuth consent prompts, suspicious URLs, and impersonation of platforms like AWS, Azure, Microsoft, and Google, but consistently failed at social-context deception and identity verification across both models.
  • The article distinguishes agent phishing, which abuses the trust the agent gives a plausible request, from indirect prompt injection, which embeds malicious instructions in data the model consumes, noting both fit Simon Willison's lethal trifecta of private data access, untrusted content, and outbound send capability.
  • Recommended defenses are architectural rather than prompt-based, including treating the agents.md file as a security control, blocking the agent from sending outbound mail to unknown addresses, segmenting connector access by inbound trust level, and requiring a human in the loop for high-privilege actions like credential forwarding.

Hackers pose as women seeking romance to spy on Russian soldiers — May Read

A newly discovered cyber espionage group called SiribClone has been targeting Russian military personnel by posing as women seeking romance or volunteers on Telegram, tricking them into installing spyware (SafeLoveStealer for Android, SiribGrabber for desktops) or entering credentials on phishing sites. The campaign steals files, location data, communications and hijacks Telegram accounts to gather battlefield intelligence, though researchers have not attributed it to any specific country or known threat actor.

  • A previously undocumented cyber espionage group, dubbed SiribClone by Russian cybersecurity firm F6, has been active since at least summer 2025 and primarily targets Russian armed forces personnel stationed in border regions and combat zones.
  • The hackers impersonate women seeking romantic relationships or volunteers offering humanitarian aid to initiate contact with servicemen on Telegram, then persuade them to download malicious apps or enter their Telegram credentials on spoofed websites.
  • The group uses Android spyware named SafeLoveStealer, which can steal photos, videos, documents and location data while remotely activating the device's microphone to record conversations, as well as desktop malware called SiribGrabber designed to steal files.
  • Researchers discovered an internal management platform named Kontur that stores stolen Telegram sessions and lets operators review intercepted messages, with internal notes referencing military ranks, unit designations, locations and operational status.
  • F6 did not attribute the campaign to any specific country or known threat actor.

Meta to Use Off-Site Business Data for Feed and AI Personalization — May Read

Meta announced that starting next month it will expand its use of activity data shared by other businesses—previously used only for targeted ads—to also personalize users' Feed content and AI chatbot responses across the U.S. and several other countries. The company says it is not collecting new data and is consolidating user controls into the 'Activity from other businesses' setting, which can be turned off to limit personalization to on-platform activity.

  • Meta announced on Tuesday that it will use information shared by other businesses about people's off-site activity—such as games played or purchases made on other websites—to personalize users' Feed content and AI chatbot responses, expanding beyond its existing use of this data for targeted ads.
  • Meta stated that it is not collecting any new data as part of this update and that users can decide how the information is used for personalization.
  • Meta is consolidating its controls by expanding the 'Activity from other businesses' setting (formerly 'Activity information from ad partners') and discontinuing the 'Your activity off Meta technologies' setting.
  • If users decline to allow off-site data for personalization, the content they see will instead be based on their on-platform activity such as liking a reel or post.
  • The change is expected to go into effect starting next month in the U.S. and several other countries, including the U.K., Brazil, Thailand, South Africa, Turkey, South Korea, Ecuador, Nigeria, and Kenya.

OpenClaw AI agent found falling for phishing attacks, spills user data — May Read

Varonis researchers tested an OpenClaw-based AI email agent against classic phishing tactics and found it leaked highly sensitive data—including AWS and database credentials and CRM customer exports—when attackers impersonated trusted staff with urgent requests, even under a strict configuration. While the agent reliably detected malicious URLs, fake login pages, and rogue OAuth apps, it failed at verifying sender identity, prompting recommendations for mandatory identity checks, restricted data access, and human approval for high-risk actions.

  • Varonis Threat Labs created an OpenClaw AI email agent named Pinchy, connected it to a Gmail inbox, browser tools, Google Workspace APIs, and fabricated sensitive company data, then tested it against classic phishing simulations using Google Gemini 3.1 Pro and OpenAI GPT-5.4.
  • In two of four simulated attacks, the agent emailed AWS IAM keys, database credentials, and SSH access details to an external Gmail account, and sent a CRM export with customer records and revenue data, all without verifying the sender's identity.
  • Both the generic and strict configuration profiles failed in the first two scenarios because the identity verification step collapsed when requests appeared operationally urgent.
  • The agent successfully detected suspicious URLs, fake login pages, and a malicious Google OAuth application disguised as a timesheet platform, with Gemini showing greater willingness to interact and GPT-5.4 taking a more cautious posture.
  • Varonis recommends that agents be required to verify sender identities, be blocked from emailing new external recipients without approval, have limited internal data access, and require human approval for high-risk actions like credential sharing and financial data requests.

Agentic Runtime Security: When Patching Can’t Keep Up. — May Read

Anthropic's Project Glasswing has shown that AI can now discover critical vulnerabilities far faster than vendors can patch them, with exploitation often beginning a week before a fix even exists. Mitiga argues this flips security from a patching problem to a runtime defense problem, requiring behavioral detection and containment across cloud, SaaS, identity, and AI to cover exposure windows that patching can no longer close.

  • Anthropic's Project Glasswing reported that roughly 50 partners used Mythos Preview to find more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities, but of 530 disclosed bugs only 75 had been patched, demonstrating that the security bottleneck has shifted from finding flaws to fixing them.
  • Anthropic expects that within 6 to 12 months many other AI companies will have Mythos-class models, some potentially released without safeguards, making the vulnerability-discovery capability widely available across the industry.
  • In 2026, exploitation often begins about a week before a patch is released, and in roughly 60% of breaches a patch already existed but was not deployed in time, meaning the unpatched exposure window is where attacks now land.
  • Real-world cases including Oracle E-Business Suite (CVE-2025-61882), the TanStack/GitHub Actions supply chain chain, and Google's confirmed first in-the-wild AI-assisted 0-day show exploitation reaching production before patching is possible.
  • Mitiga promotes Agentic Runtime Security, powered by Helios AIDR and its Cloud Security Data Lake, which uses behavioral detection, attack reconstruction, and containment across cloud, SaaS, identity, and AI as a compensating control that posture tools like SSPM and ITDR cannot provide.

npm Tooling Bug Incorrectly Marks One-Character Packages as Security Holders — May Read

A bug in npm's tooling mistakenly marked several one-character packages (such as c, r, w, and -) as security holders by adding 0.0.1-security placeholder versions and moving their latest dist-tag, though there was no actual package compromise. npm has since rolled back the changes and fixed the affected package versions.

  • A bug in npm's tooling incorrectly applied security-holder metadata to multiple one-character packages, including letters, numbers, and the dash package, by assigning 0.0.1-security or 0.0.1-security.0 versions and moving the latest dist-tag to the security placeholder.
  • npm confirmed the markings were not intentional, stating it was caused by a tooling bug and a mistake, and that they were working on rolling it back.
  • Socket found no public evidence based on registry metadata that the affected one-character packages had been compromised, and older package versions remained available throughout the incident.
  • Developers who depend on affected packages were advised to check their lockfiles (package-lock.json, yarn.lock, pnpm-lock.yaml) for 0.0.1-security or 0.0.1-security.0 entries captured during the affected window.
  • In an update on June 9, 2026, npm confirmed the affected package versions had been fixed and the one-character packages no longer appeared as security holders.

CISA to transform how it assesses cyber vulnerabilities and risks, Andersen says — Optional

CISA is overhauling how it prioritizes cyber vulnerabilities and risks, releasing a binding operational directive that shifts federal agencies away from patching everything quickly toward a risk-based approach that focuses on factors like internet exposure, known exploited vulnerabilities, and exploitability. Acting Director Nick Andersen also said the agency will work more granularly with critical infrastructure operators and plans to hire over 300 new employees to address staffing shortages.

  • CISA Acting Director Nick Andersen announced that the agency plans to overhaul how it assesses cyber vulnerabilities and threats, prioritizing some systems and critical infrastructure elements over others to be more effective amid spiking risks.
  • A binding operational directive being released Wednesday will direct federal agencies to change their vulnerability management protocols, moving away from the historical approach of applying patches as quickly as possible toward a risk-based focus that considers whether an asset is internet exposed, aligns to a known exploited vulnerability, or is automatable in its exploitation.
  • Andersen said the directive will address whether patching windows need to be shortened and by how much, and that CISA will also work more closely with critical infrastructure entities on prioritizing their responses to cyber threats.
  • Andersen criticized CISA's existing Section 9 protocol as not effective enough, saying the agency needs to move from broad intelligence conversations to a 'fine grade' approach focused on specific functions and assets, such as ensuring a bank's bulk payment system is solid rather than worrying about a single branch.
  • Despite being constrained by a government shutdown and mass layoffs, CISA plans to bring on over 300 new employees, with 180 hired by the end of the month, initially focused on infrastructure security, emergency communications, and state cybersecurity coordinators.

User-to-User Authentication: Down the Rabbit Hole – Part 1 — Optional

This first-of-two blog posts dissects how Kerberos user-to-user (U2U) authentication works internally, explaining that it encrypts service tickets with a hosting user's TGT session key to enable peer-to-peer authentication for accounts without an SPN. It shows that Windows only implements the client-initiated U2U scenario (notably in RDP with NLA), and that the same session-key primitives underpinning U2U also enable the UnPAC-the-Hash attack for recovering NT hashes via PKINIT-issued certificates.

  • Kerberos user-to-user (U2U) authentication solves peer-to-peer scenarios where a service runs under an account that has no SPN, by encrypting the service ticket with the hosting user's TGT session key rather than a service account's long-term key, using the ENC-TKT-IN-SKEY flag in a modified TGS-REQ that includes the target's TGT in the additional-tickets field.
  • Handing out TGTs is safe because they are encrypted with the randomly-generated KRBTGT long-term key, and a TGT is unusable without its associated session key, which is why Rubeus exports tickets as KRB-CRED structures containing both the ticket and the session key.
  • Although the draft RFC defines three U2U scenarios, the authors found that Windows only consistently implements the first (client-initiated) scenario, as the KDC_ERR_MUST_USE_USER2USER error does not exist in kerberos.dll and there is no per-account policy attribute to force U2U.
  • RDP with Network Level Authentication (NLA) is the most common legitimate use of U2U, where CredSSP calls InitializeSecurityContext with the ISC_REQ_USE_SESSION_KEY flag to obtain a fresh, non-cacheable session key used to securely deliver the client's plaintext credentials.
  • The UnPAC-the-Hash attack reuses U2U primitives: after obtaining a user's certificate, an attacker performs a PKINIT exchange to derive the AS reply key and TGT session key, requests a U2U service ticket for themselves, and decrypts the PAC's PAC_CREDENTIAL_INFO structure to recover the user's NT hash for Pass-the-Hash.

XBOW tests Anthropic's Mythos Preview for offensive security — Optional

XBOW evaluated Anthropic's unreleased Mythos Preview model and found it to be a major leap in offensive security capabilities, especially at source-code auditing, native-code analysis, and reverse engineering, while it remains weaker at live-site exploit validation and judgment tasks. Despite its power, the model is expensive (reportedly 5x an Opus model) and not always cost-effective, so XBOW recommends pairing it with live-site validation tooling and other models rather than relying on it alone.

  • XBOW received early access to Anthropic's Mythos Preview model roughly three months before publication and tested it both inside Claude Code and as a raw API engine for XBOW's agents using benchmarks, workflows, and interactive use.
  • Mythos Preview demonstrated a major advance in vulnerability discovery, cutting false negatives by 42% compared to Opus 4.6 on XBOW's web exploit benchmark (and 55% when given the site's source code), and showed unprecedented token-for-token precision at homing in on vulnerabilities.
  • The model is especially strong at reading source code and excels in native-code vulnerability discovery and reverse engineering, including finding more real Chromium bugs with fewer false positives and identifying true positives in V8 sandbox work.
  • Its judgment was mixed and overly literal, scoring only 77.8% on XBOW's command safety benchmark versus 81.2% for Opus 4.6 and 90.1% for Haiku 4.5, indicating it needs precise prompts, explicit threat models, and validation infrastructure to be reliable.
  • Mythos Preview is reportedly 5x as expensive as an Opus model and is not best-in-class on cost efficiency, leading XBOW to conclude that depending on the task it may be more sensible to let a cheaper model like GPT-5.5 run longer than to use Mythos Preview once.

What Trump’s AI Executive Order Means for Healthcare Sector — Optional

President Trump's new AI executive order barely mentions healthcare, but experts like Health-ISAC's Errol Weiss say it could benefit the sector by improving speed and coordination against AI-empowered attackers—provided its outputs translate into timely, actionable information sharing through established ISAC channels.

  • President Donald Trump issued an executive order on artificial intelligence that barely mentions healthcare, but experts believe it could positively impact the critical infrastructure sector if implemented effectively.
  • Errol Weiss, chief security officer at Health-ISAC, said the executive order's biggest opportunity for healthcare is speed and coordination in staying ahead of attackers who are leveraging AI to compress the time between vulnerability discovery and exploitation.
  • Weiss emphasized that execution of the order matters, and the sector will benefit most if review/benchmarking outputs, vulnerability clearinghouse activity, and tool access translate into timely, actionable information sharing through established ISAC channels.
  • The desired outcome is measurable resilience improvements so that cyber incidents do not become prolonged critical infrastructure outages that impact national security.

New in the Threat Center: Compromised Components, Now Available via API — Optional

StepSecurity has launched a new API endpoint that lets Enterprise customers programmatically retrieve the compromised OSS components for a specific supply chain threat incident, returning structured data like package name, version, severity, and verification status. This enables teams to automate incident response, enrich SIEM workflows, and cross-reference incidents against their own dependency inventories without manual dashboard checks.

  • StepSecurity introduced a new API endpoint, GET /github/{owner}/threat-intel/incidents/{incidentId}/compromised-components, that returns every compromised OSS component tied to a specific threat incident, including ecosystem type, affected version, severity, verification status, and a threat description.
  • The endpoint complements StepSecurity's existing supply chain defenses, which include real-time detection events streamed to SIEM platforms via S3 and webhooks, the Threat Center dashboard, Pull Request checks like the NPM Package Compromised Updates Check, and the Secure Registry that enforces controls at install time.
  • Teams can use the endpoint to automate incident response by pulling the full list of compromised components from an incident identifier, enrich SIEM workflows with typed component data, and cross-reference incidents against their own SBOM or lockfiles to quickly determine exposure.
  • The same StepSecurity Threat Intelligence systems behind this feature previously uncovered the tj-actions and nx supply chain incidents and detect active compromises within minutes.
  • The Threat Center and the compromised components endpoint are available to StepSecurity Enterprise customers, who can generate an API token from their dashboard to query real incidents.

What Healthcare Leaders Need to Know About Cybersecurity in 2026-2027 — Optional

Health-ISAC's Errol Weiss warns that healthcare organizations remain prime cyberattack targets due to life-critical systems, sensitive patient data, and fragile, under-resourced networks, with ransomware posing growing risks in 2026-2027. He urges healthcare leaders to prioritize information sharing, incident preparedness, and treating cybersecurity as a patient safety issue.

  • Errol Weiss, Chief Security Officer at Health-ISAC, argues that healthcare is a perfect target for cyberattacks because it combines life-critical systems, highly sensitive patient data, and valuable clinical research running on complex, fragile networks in under-resourced environments.
  • The article focuses on evolving healthcare cybersecurity risks for the 2026-2027 period.
  • It places particular emphasis on the ramifications of ransomware in the healthcare sector.
  • The piece highlights the importance of information sharing, incident preparedness, and treating cybersecurity as a patient safety issue.
  • It is intended to help healthcare leaders understand the impacts of cyber threats on care delivery, operations, and organizational resilience.

Permiso Recognized as a Challenger and Outperformer in the 2026 GigaOm Identity Threat Detection & Response (ITDR) Radar — Optional

Permiso announced it was recognized as both a Challenger and an Outperformer in the 2026 GigaOm Radar for Identity Threat Detection and Response (ITDR), marking its second consecutive year in the report.

  • Permiso was recognized in the 2026 GigaOm Radar for Identity Threat Detection and Response (ITDR) as both a Challenger and an Outperformer.
  • This marks Permiso's second consecutive year being included in the GigaOm ITDR report.
  • The article notes that ITDR has become a central category for how enterprises defend themselves, because the identities that run modern environments have also become the easiest way into them.

item.title: NFCShare Android Banking Malware and CISA OT Zero Trust Guidance
item.description: This digest covers two distinct but operationally relevant developments: an Android banking malware campaign abusing GitHub for distribution, and evolving federal guidance on applying zero trust principles to operational technology environments. The reporting highlights continued pressure on...
item.content:encoded: This digest covers two distinct but operationally relevant developments: an Android banking malware campaign abusing GitHub for distribution, and evolving federal guidance on applying zero trust principles to operational technology environments. The reporting highlights continued pressure on financial fraud defenses from mobile malware, while also reflecting the growing security complexity created by IT/OT convergence, remote access, cloud integration, and third-party support models. New NFCShare Android malware variants are being distributed as fake updates for legitimate banking applications hosted on GitHub. The campaign targets customers of multiple banks and financial institutions across Europe, with impersonated applications primarily tied to banks in Italy and Spain. Since April 10, the GitHub repository used in the campaign reportedly hosted 56 unique APKs masquerading as banking app updates. NFCShare is designed to steal payment card data through social engineering and near-field communication access. The malware prompts victims to place payment cards near the infected phone’s NFC chip, then uses Android’s IsoDep interface and EMV commands to read card information. The collected data is exfiltrated to a command-and-control host over WebSocket, enabling attackers to harvest payment card details from compromised devices. The newer samples also include anti-analysis characteristics aimed at frustrating automated inspection. According to the reporting, malformed APK packaging and poisoned file paths are used to disrupt static analysis tooling. This evolution indicates that the operators are adapting their distribution and packaging methods in response to malware detection and analysis workflows. CISA’s recent guidance emphasizes that zero trust principles cannot be copied directly from enterprise IT into operational technology environments without adaptation. The guidance notes that OT deployments often involve legacy assets, segmented networks, safety-critical operations, and operational risk tolerances that differ substantially from traditional IT systems. These constraints shape how federal agencies are expected to interpret and apply zero trust concepts in environments supporting critical infrastructure operations. The guidance identifies visibility, segmentation, least privilege, secure remote access, and resilience as core zero trust principles for OT settings. The article also describes a broader convergence trend in which federal OT environments are increasingly connected with enterprise IT, cloud services, and emerging AI agents. Remote access and third-party support are described as standard elements of modern OT operations, increasing the importance of architectures that account for both cyber risk and operational continuity. The reporting presents Netskope’s view that security architectures must extend zero trust controls across hybrid IT and OT environments while preserving safety and availability requirements. This reflects a wider market and policy focus on adapting identity, access, segmentation, and monitoring models to industrial and mission-critical systems. The theme is less about a single incident and more about the continuing shift in how government and critical infrastructure operators conceptualize OT security as connectivity expands.

item.title: Shai-Hulud Supply-Chain Attacks, CVE-2026-23111 Exploits, and NSO Phishing Activity
item.description: The coverage window was dominated by identity-centric compromise, software supply-chain abuse, and post-exploitation paths that turn trusted access into broad data exposure. Several reports show attackers targeting developer ecosystems, SaaS integrations, and legacy infrastructure rather than...
item.content:encoded: The coverage window was dominated by identity-centric compromise, software supply-chain abuse, and post-exploitation paths that turn trusted access into broad data exposure. Several reports show attackers targeting developer ecosystems, SaaS integrations, and legacy infrastructure rather than relying only on conventional endpoint malware. Public exploit availability for a Linux kernel privilege-escalation flaw and renewed commercial spyware phishing activity add near-term operational relevance for defenders tracking exposed systems and high-risk users. Two reports describe new Shai-Hulud activity against open-source software ecosystems. In one case, attackers compromised the LeonOstrez GitHub account and force-pushed a credential-stealing payload to the Pythagora-io/gpt-pilot repository’s main branch, which lacked branch protection. The payload included a Python loader, an obfuscated JavaScript file named runtime.bin, and import-triggered execution through core/telemetry/init.py; its collection targets included AWS keys, npm tokens, GitHub secrets, Kubernetes service accounts, Vault tokens, and SSH keys. A separate Shai-Hulud campaign trojanized 19 science-focused PyPI packages across 37 malicious releases, including bioinformatics tools such as Dynamo, Spateo, CoolBox, U-FISH, and Napari-UFISH. Those packages used -setup.pth execution and an obfuscated index.js payload that downloaded the Bun runtime and searched for GitHub, npm, PyPI, RubyGems, JFrog, cloud, Kubernetes, Docker, SSH, configuration, and shell-history secrets. The GitHub incident was notable because CI tooling blocked execution paths twice: first through a ruff format failure and then through a ruff check import-order violation. Cyera’s analysis of ShinyHunters frames a related but broader pattern: attackers abusing legitimate access through stolen credentials, session tokens, OAuth grants, SaaS integrations, and native export APIs. The report links this playbook to Snowflake-related intrusions, Vercel and Context.ai, Salesloft and Gainsight integration compromises, and the April 2026 Vimeo dataset leak, which reportedly exposed identity, Salesforce, product analytics, content metadata, marketing attribution, and API data layers. SoFi confirmed that customers of SoFi Securities Hong Kong were affected by unauthorized access to a third-party vendor database. The company said it discovered the incident on April 30, 2026, but had not yet determined which specific data categories were involved. SoFi declined to identify the vendor, disclose the number of affected customers, or say whether extortion was involved. The SoFi disclosure aligns with the broader trend described in the ShinyHunters reporting: a single vendor, integration, or identity can provide access to data spread across business platforms. In both cases, the central issue is not necessarily direct compromise of a primary application, but the exposure created by trusted external systems and the data privileges granted to them. CVE-2026-23111, a Linux kernel use-after-free in nftables, drew attention because public exploit details are now available. The flaw can allow an unprivileged local user to escalate to root and potentially escape containers on systems using nftables with unprivileged user namespaces enabled. Researchers demonstrated or reproduced exploitation across Debian Bookworm, Debian Trixie, Ubuntu 22.04 LTS, Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, and RHEL 10, while major Linux vendors are tracking or providing fixes. A NetSPI penetration-testing case study showed how legacy and modern authentication infrastructure can combine into a full Active Directory compromise path. The tester found unauthenticated Apache NiFi servers and achieved root command execution, then discovered cleartext credentials on a Linux host. The environment also exposed Network Information Service password hashes without authentication, and a cracked privileged password led to access to a Duo Authentication Proxy host containing AD service credentials and a RADIUS shared secret. The case study culminated in decrypted Duo proxy authentication traffic and relay of an MSSQL service account that belonged to Domain Admins. That relay enabled creation of a new domain admin account and DCSync, illustrating how Linux infrastructure, NIS, MFA proxy configuration, RADIUS secrets, and Windows domain privileges can collapse into a single escalation chain. WhatsApp said it disrupted spear-phishing campaigns allegedly linked to NSO Group after investigating user reports of social engineering. Meta said attackers attempted to lure targets to malicious external links, removed NSO-linked test accounts and groups, and published indicators including ikhwancast[.]com, ghazacast[.]com, and fr24cast[.]com. The activity was described as similar to previously reported one-click phishing campaigns associated with NSO Group. Apple announced an Apple Intelligence-powered capability for iOS 27 that will automatically update eligible weak or compromised passwords in the Passwords app and Safari. Apple said the feature will use on-device models and Private Cloud Compute, which the company says does not store users’ personal data or make it accessible to Apple or others. The United Kingdom separately gave major technology companies three months to create smartphone and tablet safeguards that detect and block nude images of children. The Home Office said the controls would operate at device level across apps and services without data collection, monitoring, or reporting, and warned that legislation, fines, and potential executive liability may follow if companies do not comply.

item.title: Check Point CVE-2026-50751 Exploitation, UniFi Root Chain, and NSO WhatsApp Phishing
item.description: This digest window was dominated by high-impact vulnerability reporting, including active exploitation of a Check Point VPN flaw and newly disclosed critical attack paths in UniFi OS Server and Gogs. Reporting also highlighted continued spyware-linked targeting of messaging users, Russia-aligned...
item.content:encoded: This digest window was dominated by high-impact vulnerability reporting, including active exploitation of a Check Point VPN flaw and newly disclosed critical attack paths in UniFi OS Server and Gogs. Reporting also highlighted continued spyware-linked targeting of messaging users, Russia-aligned influence and surveillance activity, and several major data exposure incidents affecting healthcare, humanitarian, and consumer security services. Check Point warned of active exploitation of CVE-2026-50751, a critical logic-flow vulnerability affecting Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments using deprecated IKEv1 configurations. The flaw can allow unauthenticated remote attackers to bypass user authentication and establish VPN access without a valid user password under specific legacy configuration conditions. Check Point said suspicious activity was first observed on June 4, with earliest exploitation dating to May 7, and noted one post-exploitation case associated with a Qilin ransomware affiliate. Ubiquiti UniFi OS Server 5.0.6 and earlier was reported vulnerable to a chained unauthenticated root remote code execution path involving CVE-2026-34908, CVE-2026-34909, and CVE-2026-34910. Bishop Fox validated the chain against a live instance and described an authentication bypass caused by differences between raw URI validation and Nginx normalized URI routing, followed by command injection through a package-update endpoint and root escalation via passwordless sudo privileges. Gogs released version 0.14.3 to address a critical argument injection zero-day affecting all releases up to and including 0.14.2 and 0.15.0+dev. Rapid7 reported that authenticated non-admin users could exploit the flaw to compromise servers, access private repositories, steal credentials, move laterally, and tamper with hosted source code, with default configurations increasing exposure because open registration and unrestricted repository creation are enabled by default. Meta and WhatsApp accused NSO Group of conducting new spear-phishing activity against WhatsApp users in violation of a permanent injunction barring the company from using WhatsApp as an attack vector. The reported campaign used social engineering to drive victims to malicious external websites, and Meta said it removed associated test accounts and groups. Domains linked to the activity included fr24cast[.]com, ghazacast[.]com, and ikhwancast[.]com. Check Point Research also described a malware distribution ecosystem impersonating open-source tools and delivering RemusStealer, AnimateClipper, and a newly identified loader named SessionGate. In parallel, Dashlane disclosed an attack affecting fewer than 20 users in which threat actors brute-forced two-factor codes to register unauthorized devices and download encrypted password vaults. DentaQuest suffered a data breach after ShinyHunters leaked exfiltrated data tied to an estimated 2.6 million accounts. The exposed information reportedly included names, email addresses, government identifiers, and health insurance details, placing the incident within the broader pattern of healthcare and insurance-sector data theft. The United Nations World Food Programme disclosed unauthorized access to its Gaza self-registration application, affecting approximately 600,000 Palestinian households. Reported exposed data included names, identification numbers, mobile numbers, and location information, creating heightened sensitivity due to the humanitarian context and the population affected. Researchers reported that Kremlin-linked actors spent eight months targeting Armenia’s parliamentary election through the Matryoshka influence campaign. The activity used fabricated news reports, manipulated videos, bot amplification, fake media sites, impersonated journalists, and interconnected influence networks to promote narratives accusing Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan of corruption, anti-Russian plotting, and serious criminal conduct. Armenian authorities also reported false bomb threats sent to polling stations from foreign phone numbers and email addresses. Russia’s Ministry of Digital Development published updated technical standards for SORM, the country’s digital interception and surveillance system. The new rules expand searchable data categories to include passport information, tax identifiers, usernames, domains, URLs, device identifiers, corporate records, and geographic coordinates, while applying compliance burdens to telecom operators, cloud providers, hosting services, banks, universities, and large organizations operating internet infrastructure. SpecterOps published updated AzureHound least-privilege documentation that narrows required Microsoft Graph and Azure RBAC permissions. The validated configuration replaces broad permissions such as Directory.Read.All and RoleManagement.Read.All with eight granular Microsoft Graph application permissions and substitutes the built-in Azure Reader role with a custom AzureHound Reader role containing 16 specific read actions. BleepingComputer covered Wazuh Cloud as a managed, cloud-native version of the open-source Wazuh platform intended to reduce SIEM and XDR operational complexity. The service is described as handling backend maintenance, patches, rule updates, threat intelligence updates, scaling, preconfigured dashboards, and AI-generated weekly security assessments across endpoint, container, and cloud workloads.
